meta.title: Requests — Architecture
meta.tagline: HTTP for Humans — how a requests.get() actually happens

nodes.label: Public API
nodes.summary: The friendly one-liners everyone imports: requests.get(), post(), put()...
nodes.detail:
This is the famous surface of the library — and it is almost empty. Every function (get, post, head, ...) funnels into requests.request(), which creates a fresh, throwaway Session inside a context manager for each call. All real behavior lives in the Session engine.

Practical consequence: calling the module-level API in a loop forfeits connection pooling — each call builds and tears down a Session. Long-lived code should hold its own Session.

nodes.label: Session Engine
nodes.summary: The orchestrator: merges settings, prepares requests, routes them to adapters, follows redirects.
nodes.detail:
Session.request() is the heart of the library. It merges per-call arguments with session-level defaults (headers, auth, proxies, cookies), wraps them in a Request, prepares it into a PreparedRequest, and hands it to Session.send().

send() picks a transport adapter by longest URL-prefix match (mount() registers adapters — by default https:// and http:// both map to HTTPAdapter), dispatches the request, resolves redirects via the resolve_redirects generator, persists cookies, and fires response hooks.

nodes.label: Models
nodes.summary: Request → PreparedRequest → Response: the three data objects every call flows through.
nodes.detail:
Request is the user's intent; PreparedRequest is the fully-baked HTTP message — URL parsed and percent-encoded, headers merged, body encoded (form / JSON / multipart via prepare_body), auth applied, Cookie header rendered. This split is deliberate: you can inspect or modify a PreparedRequest before sending it.

Response wraps the raw urllib3 reply: lazy content / text / json(), raise_for_status(), streaming via iter_content(), and a link back to the request and its redirect history.

nodes.label: Transport Adapters
nodes.summary: HTTPAdapter: the bridge from requests' world to urllib3's connection pools.
nodes.detail:
HTTPAdapter owns a urllib3 PoolManager — this is where connection pooling, TLS verification, client certs, retries, and proxy handling are configured. Its send() performs the actual network I/O, and build_response() converts the raw urllib3 reply into a requests Response.

The adapter interface is the library's main extension point: mount a custom adapter on a Session to change transport behavior (custom TLS contexts, testing mocks, retry policies) without touching user code.

nodes.label: urllib3
nodes.summary: The third-party HTTP engine underneath every request.
nodes.detail: urllib3 does the low-level work requests deliberately hides: connection pools, TLS handshakes, timeouts, chunked transfer, low-level retries. Requests' value proposition is the humane API on top — but the power of urllib3 remains reachable through HTTPAdapter configuration.

nodes.label: Auth
nodes.summary: Callable auth objects: Basic, Proxy, and stateful Digest authentication.
nodes.detail:
Auth in requests is just a callable that mutates a PreparedRequest. HTTPBasicAuth and HTTPProxyAuth set a header at prepare time and are done.

HTTPDigestAuth is the interesting one: it is stateful and registers a response hook — when the server answers 401 with a challenge, the hook computes the digest and replays the request. A neat example of the hooks system used internally.

nodes.label: Cookies
nodes.summary: RequestsCookieJar: dict-like API over stdlib cookiejar semantics, persisted across a Session.
nodes.detail: RequestsCookieJar merges two worlds: the ergonomic dict-like access users want, and the RFC-compliant domain/path matching of stdlib http.cookiejar. Helper functions extract cookies from each response into the jar and render the Cookie header at prepare time — which is how cookies survive redirects and persist across all requests made through one Session.

nodes.label: Hooks
nodes.summary: A minimal event system — one 'response' event, dispatched over user callbacks.
nodes.detail: The entire file is ~30 lines. dispatch_hook() runs registered callbacks over each response, letting users observe or even replace responses. Digest authentication is built on this same mechanism, which is a good signal the abstraction is pulling its weight despite its size.

nodes.label: Exceptions
nodes.summary: The RequestsException tree: users handle requests' types, never urllib3's.
nodes.detail: A hierarchy rooted at RequestException: ConnectionError, Timeout (split into connect/read), HTTPError, TooManyRedirects, and friends. Adapters translate urllib3's exceptions into these, so calling code depends only on requests' error surface — a deliberate insulation layer around the external engine.

nodes.label: Utilities
nodes.summary: Shared helpers: URL requoting, proxy env detection, encoding guesses, CaseInsensitiveDict.
nodes.detail: The grab-bag every module leans on: URL requoting and validation, proxy environment detection (get_environ_proxies), header parsing, content-encoding guessing, and CaseInsensitiveDict (in structures.py) which backs all header handling. compat.py is a remnant of the Python 2/3 era, kept thin.

edges.label: one-shot Session
edges.summary: Every module-level call creates a throwaway Session.
edges.detail: requests.request() opens with sessions.Session() as session: and delegates immediately. This keeps the one-liner API stateless — at the cost of a new connection pool per call.

edges.label: prepare_request()
edges.summary: The Session turns a user Request into a fully-baked PreparedRequest.
edges.detail: Session.prepare_request() merges session defaults (headers, auth, cookies, params) with per-call arguments, then calls PreparedRequest.prepare() — which encodes the URL, body, and headers into the exact bytes that will go on the wire.

edges.label: prepare_auth()
edges.summary: Auth callables mutate the PreparedRequest during preparation.
edges.detail: PreparedRequest.prepare_auth() invokes the auth object as a callable, letting it attach Authorization headers (Basic) or register hooks for challenge-response flows (Digest).

edges.label: prepare_cookies()
edges.summary: The Cookie header is rendered from the jar at prepare time.
edges.detail: PreparedRequest.prepare_cookies() uses the cookiejar machinery to compute which cookies match the target URL's domain and path, and renders them into a single Cookie header.

edges.label: send(PreparedRequest)
edges.summary: Session.send() picks an adapter by URL prefix and dispatches.
edges.detail: Session.get_adapter() does a longest-prefix match over mounted adapters (by default https:// and http:// → HTTPAdapter). This indirection is what makes transport behavior pluggable per-Session.

edges.label: urlopen()
edges.summary: The actual network I/O happens inside urllib3's pooled connections.
edges.detail: HTTPAdapter.send() resolves a connection from its PoolManager and calls urlopen() with the prepared method, URL, headers and body. Timeouts, TLS and low-level retries are all applied here.

edges.label: build_response()
edges.summary: The raw urllib3 reply is wrapped into a requests Response.
edges.detail: HTTPAdapter.build_response() copies status, headers, and the raw stream into a Response, decodes cookies into the jar, and links the response back to its originating PreparedRequest.

edges.label: persist cookies
edges.summary: After each response (and each redirect hop), cookies are extracted into the Session's jar.
edges.detail: extract_cookies_to_jar() is called in Session.send() and inside the redirect loop, which is how login cookies set on hop 1 are available on hop 2 — and on every later request through the same Session.

edges.label: dispatch_hook('response')
edges.summary: User callbacks run over every response before it is returned.
edges.detail: After a response is built, Session.send() calls dispatch_hook(). A hook may inspect the response or return a replacement — digest auth uses exactly this to transparently retry after a 401 challenge.

edges.label: translate errors
edges.summary: urllib3 exceptions are converted into requests' own exception tree.
edges.detail: HTTPAdapter.send() catches urllib3's MaxRetryError, ConnectTimeoutError, SSLError etc. and re-raises them as requests.exceptions.* — so user code never needs to import urllib3 to handle failures.

edges.label: raise_for_status()
edges.detail: Response.raise_for_status() raises HTTPError for client and server error status codes. It is opt-in — requests never raises on HTTP error status by itself, a deliberate and sometimes-debated design choice.

edges.label: env & URL helpers
edges.summary: Proxy environment detection, redirect target resolution, header merging.
edges.detail: The Session engine leans on utilities for environment-based proxy configuration (merge_environment_settings), URL requoting during redirects, and CaseInsensitiveDict for all header handling.
